problem_summary: 2024 MCM Problem A (continuous) asked teams to model the adaptive, environment-dependent sex ratio of sea lampreys: (1) the effect on the larger ecosystem when sex ratio correlates with population size, (2) the advantages/disadvantages of this trait for the lamprey population itself, (3) its impact on ecosystem stability, and (4) whether lampreys' variable sex ratio benefits other species such as parasites. This O-award paper (team 2425397) builds a single age-structured discrete population dynamics model and re-uses it across all four tasks by changing initial conditions, perturbation terms, and added populations.
models_used: Age-structured discrete population dynamics model (Leslie-matrix-style cohort recursion): lampreys split into 7 yearly age classes, ages 1-4 larvae (sexless, plankton feeders) and 5-7 adults (sexed, fish parasites), each cohort advanced by a per-capita growth/mortality difference equation X_{i+1,t+1} relative to X_{i,t}
Density-dependent (Logistic-type) growth for prey/feeder populations: x_{t+1} via r_x(1 - x_t/K_x) with carrying capacity K_x, extended with predation/competition loss terms

Multi-species Lotka-Volterra-style competition/parasitism web: larvae vs larval-competitor B_t over plankton x_t; adults vs adult-competitor C_t over fish y_t; pairwise intra- and inter-species competition coefficients (k_i, k-tilde_i, k_t, etc.)

Sex-ratio submodel: female proportion M_t modeled as a saturating rational (Michaelis-Menten-like) function of adult abundance, M_t = (e1 + e2*sum X_j)/(e3 + e4*sum X_j), capturing the food/abundance-dependent female fraction (44-50% when food abundant, ~22% when scarce)
Reproduction (recruitment) rule: one-time semelparous spawning at age 7, X_{1,t+1} = alpha * M_t * X_{7,t}
External artificial-intervention (harvesting) term F subtracted from cohort growth rates to model human pressure on migratory spawners
Impulse/pulse perturbation terms (E for lamprey crash, F for plankton spike) injected at a chosen year t0 to test resistance and resilience stability
Parasite extension (Task 4): added parasite population P_t with host-density-dependent growth and a death-risk coupling term D*P_t on adult lampreys (host-parasite dynamics)
key_techniques: Single unified model reused across all 4 tasks - only initial conditions, perturbation terms, and one added population (parasite) change between tasks, giving strong internal coherence
Biology-driven discretization: choosing a 7-year yearly time step matched to the lamprey's exactly-7-year semelparous lifecycle, with metamorphosis at year 5 as the larva/adult split

7-year moving-average smoothing (Delta_{i,t} = (1/7) sum over tau=-3..3) to extract the equilibrium trend from the intrinsic 7-year reproductive oscillation, plus phase-plane closed-loop (limit-cycle) plots of larvae-vs-plankton and adults-vs-fish to justify that the smoothing is legitimate
Controlled A/B comparison as the central rhetorical device: every claim is shown as variable-sex-ratio vs fixed-50/50 side by side (Figures 7 vs 8, 18a vs 18b, 19a vs 19b, 21a vs 21b)

Quantified stability metrics: recovery duration t_Delta = t2 - t1 and total fluctuation degree Phi = sum |Delta_{i,t+tau} - Delta_{i,t}|/Delta_{i,t}, yielding concrete numbers (e.g. variable 23.28%/18yr vs invariant 30.41%/22yr; plankton shock 33.19%/28yr vs 49.75%/38yr)

Model validation against literature: showing the simulated female-fraction-vs-adult-abundance relationship is negatively correlated, matching Hardisty (Nature 1961), and female-fraction tracks resource level per the problem statement
Local sensitivity analysis: +/-5% one-at-a-time perturbation of M_t, alpha, F, beta, k identifying alpha and M_t as dominant (17.56% and 16.03% output swing vs <3.34% for others)
why_it_won: "From a judge's perspective the paper wins on disciplined model economy and decision-relevance rather than mathematical sophistication. It builds ONE biologically-grounded age-structured model and answers all four sub-questions with it, which reads as a coherent thesis rather than four disconnected mini-models - a common O-award discriminator. The sex-ratio mechanism (the actual crux of the problem) is modeled explicitly as a feedback loop (food -> growth rate -> sex ratio -> recruitment -> population), not bolted on. Crucially, nearly every qualitative claim is backed by a controlled variable-vs-fixed sex-ratio comparison AND a quantified metric (recovery time, % fluctuation), so the conclusion 'adaptive sex ratio increases resistance and resilience stability' is demonstrated, not asserted. The team also handled the awkward 7-year oscillation honestly - smoothing it but then validating the smoothing via limit-cycle phase plots - which shows mathematical maturity. Tying results to the real Great Lakes invasion and fishery losses gives it the real-world relevance and storytelling judges reward."
section_highlights: "Abstract: results-forward and quantitative - leads each task with concrete findings and embeds the headline stability numbers (23.28%/33.19% fluctuation, 18/28-year recovery). Section 3 (Model): clean build-up from single-cohort mortality (Eq 1-2) to competition (Eq 3) to recruitment (Eq 4-5), consolidated into two summary tables (larva-side and adult-side) that let a judge see the whole system at a glance. Figures: heavy use of paired (a)/(b) variable-vs-fixed comparisons and phase-plane validation plots. Notation table, explicit Assumptions-with-Justification format (each assumption paired with a biological rationale and a citation), and a transparent AI-use appendix (GPT-4 Turbo queries on plankton dynamics and ecosystem-stability definitions) all signal rigor. Sensitivity section isolates the two driving parameters. Strengths/Weaknesses is short but honest (admits no predation on lampreys)."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Numbers are dimensionless and on an arbitrary ~0-2 scale with no real calibration to lamprey census data - all initial values and ~30 coefficients (Table 4) are hand-set, so quantitative outputs (e.g. 23.28%) are internally-relative, not empirically validated
The sex-ratio function M_t is fit only qualitatively to one 1961 Nature reference; epsilon parameters are unexplained 'nonlinear function parameters' with no derivation or units

Equation/notation inconsistencies that judges would notice: beta_m vs beta_n used loosely, Eq 15 references a non-existent 'Equation (17)', M_t is described as 'proportion of females' in notation but used as a recruitment multiplier, and time axis is labeled both 'year' and 'day' in places (plankton outbreak 'at day 75')

Sensitivity figure data table (Fig 23) shows all perturbed curves as identical to baseline, contradicting the text's claimed 17.56%/16.03% swings - the supporting data does not visibly demonstrate the stated result
Self-admitted: no natural predation on lampreys is modeled (Assumption 3), so trophic feedback on the lamprey population is incomplete
Only local one-at-a-time +/-5% sensitivity; no global sensitivity, no parameter-interaction or uncertainty analysis
Causal claims (purifying water, benthic nutrient cycling) are asserted from literature, not produced by the model itself
reusable_patterns: One-model-many-tasks architecture: invest in a single well-justified core model, then answer each sub-question by perturbing initial conditions / adding a term / toggling a feature - far stronger than a different ad-hoc model per question
Match the time step and structure to the organism's biology: a semelparous 7-year lifecycle -> yearly age cohorts with a hard larva/adult metamorphosis split; let the lifecycle dictate discretization
Encode the problem's central mechanism as an explicit feedback loop with its own submodel (here, the resource->sex-ratio->recruitment loop) rather than treating it as an exogenous constant
When an intrinsic oscillation obscures the trend, smooth with a window equal to the natural period, then VALIDATE the smoothing with phase-plane/limit-cycle plots so reviewers trust it
A/B counterfactual as proof: to argue a trait is beneficial, always run the model with vs without that trait under identical conditions and show both curves side by side
Define explicit scalar stability metrics up front (recovery duration, summed relative fluctuation) so 'more stable' becomes a number you can tabulate and compare
Close the loop on credibility: validate at least one model relationship against a cited empirical study, and run a simple +/-x% OAT sensitivity sweep to name the few dominant parameters
Assumptions-with-justification-and-citation format: every simplifying assumption immediately followed by a biological rationale and a reference
Results-first abstract that states each task's concrete finding plus its headline number, so a triage judge gets the whole story in 30 seconds
